museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Atari's Video Computer System launches, giving the cartridge-console idea the shape most people will later remember.
museum.curatorNote: September is the richest 1977 month because the VCS launch library gives the archive a real shelf.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a very early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Atari Video Computer System launches
items.body: The VCS reaches the market and begins the long transformation of home videogames into a cartridge-library culture.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 / VCS sources place launch in September 1977.

items.title: Combat becomes the pack-in ritual
items.body: Tanks and planes make two-player home play feel social, tactical and instantly repeatable.
items.sourceNote: Combat is widely documented as the VCS pack-in.

items.title: The launch library bridges old and new
items.body: Many early cartridges are variations on arcade and dedicated-console ideas, but now they are software objects.
items.sourceNote: Atari launch-library sources list early cartridges including Combat, Air-Sea Battle and Video Olympics.

items.title: Super Bug belongs to the arcade year
items.body: Atari's scrolling driving cabinet is another 1977 sign that arcades still innovate alongside home consoles.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History lists Super Bug as 1977.

items.title: The UK impact is delayed and uneven
items.body: The VCS is historically central, but its British cultural weight grows later rather than appearing fully formed in September.
items.sourceNote: Regional launch/impact caveat.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A fuller shelf, mixing confirmed month anchors with clearly labelled year-level context.

items.title: RCA Studio II
items.body: A cartridge console with black-and-white graphics and keypad controls, historically important despite being quickly outclassed.
items.sourceNote: RCA Studio II sources place release in January 1977.

items.title: Nintendo Color TV-Game 6
items.body: Nintendo's first home console, a Japan-only dedicated TV game machine made with Mitsubishi.
items.sourceNote: Color TV-Game sources place the 6 model in June 1977.

items.title: Nintendo Color TV-Game 15
items.body: A more capable follow-up model with detachable controllers, still dedicated to Pong-style variants.
items.sourceNote: Color TV-Game sources place the 15 model in June 1977.

items.title: Atari Video Computer System
items.body: The Atari VCS launches as a cartridge system whose real cultural power will unfold over the next several years.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS / 2600 sources place launch in September 1977.

items.title: Combat
items.body: The pack-in and defining launch cartridge: tanks, planes, ricochets and one of the first living-room multiplayer rituals.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS launch-library sources list Combat as the pack-in.

items.title: Air-Sea Battle
items.body: A launch-window cartridge of simple shooting variations, showing the VCS as a menu of modes rather than one fixed game.
items.sourceNote: Atari launch-library references list Air-Sea Battle among 1977 cartridges.

items.title: Video Olympics
items.body: Dozens of Pong-style variants on cartridge, a bridge between dedicated consoles and programmable home systems.
items.sourceNote: Atari launch-library references list Video Olympics among early VCS titles.

items.title: Space Wars
items.body: Cinematronics' vector arcade adaptation of the Spacewar! idea, often cited as the first vector-graphics arcade game.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History lists Space Wars as 1977; exact month varies in secondary accounts.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, cartridges, handheld electronics and computing culture.

items.title: Atari Video Computer System
items.body: By September 1977, the VCS is the hardware object that makes cartridges feel like a home library rather than a technical curiosity.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS sources.

items.title: RCA Studio II
items.body: Historically important because it is early; commercially vulnerable because its black-and-white keypad design is quickly outpaced.
items.sourceNote: RCA Studio II sources.

items.title: Nintendo Color TV-Game
items.body: Nintendo's first home-console line is dedicated and Japan-only, but it begins a major hardware story.
items.sourceNote: Color TV-Game sources.

items.title: Vector arcade hardware
items.body: Space Wars shows the arcade moving toward sharp line-drawn images that will become central to later classics.
items.sourceNote: Space Wars sources.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: There is still no normal videogame magazine shelf. Electronics and computing magazines are the map.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online play was not a home visitor experience
items.body: For most players in September 1977, game discovery happened through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth. Networked play existed at institutional edges, not as a normal domestic habit.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later internet culture backward.

items.title: PLATO remains the hidden network
items.body: PLATO's games, messaging and shared terminals belong to the background of the era, but not to everyday UK consumer play.
items.sourceNote: PLATO context from early computer-game histories.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues and club newsletters are how players and hobbyists learn what exists.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the social feed
items.body: Scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet itself a public information system.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade culture context.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future still split between public machines and experimental home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: Arcades still feel richer than home, but home hardware is becoming more serious and more legible.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: The cartridge is the key change: a game can now be a separate object, a small plastic promise that the machine has a future.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: For a UK visitor, the story is delayed and uneven: historically important hardware appears before it becomes a normal local childhood memory.

items.title: The record is patchy because the medium is young
items.body: There are few clean launch days, few consumer reviews and no settled games press. The museum label often has to say: year-level, regional, uncertain.

Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
